title: NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Practices — Baseline Control Compliance

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(g) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(g) (Cyber-hygiene and training) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.28: NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Practices — Baseline Control Compliance.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.28' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `WinEventLog:Security` (4624 with MFA claims where available), `OktaIM2:log`, `tenable:was` or agent posture, `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv`
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv
| join asset_fqdn [
    search index=tenable sourcetype="tenable:sc:assets" earliest=-1d
    | eval mfa_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)mfa|okta.verify"),1,0)
    | eval edr_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike"),1,0)
    | stats latest(mfa_ok) as mfa_ok latest(edr_ok) as edr_ok by dns_name
    | rename dns_name as asset_fqdn
]
| eval hygiene_failures=if(mfa_ok==0,1,0) + if(edr_ok==0,1,0)
| where hygiene_failures > 0
| table asset_fqdn, business_service, mfa_ok, edr_ok, hygiene_failures
description: Basic cyber hygiene underpins all Article 21 measures. This use case scores endpoints and servers for MFA enrollment, disk encryption, EDR presence, and secure configuration baselines — rolling up to a hygiene scorecard per business service in NIS2 scope.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes tenable and sourcetypes tenable:sc:assets. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Define mandatory hygiene controls per asset class; (2) normalise hostnames between CMDB and Tenable; (3) weekly remediation campaigns for failing assets; (4) escalate chronic failures to service owners; (5) include scorecard in board security briefing. Indexes required: `index=tenable`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets`; Lookups: `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations`, `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv`.
visualization: Bar chart (failures by control), Table (worst assets), Single value (% compliant fleet), Treemap (by department). Key fields: `asset_fqdn`, `business_service`, `edr_ok`, `hygiene_failures`, `mfa_ok`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Security` (4624 with MFA claims where available), `OktaIM2:log`, `tenable:was` or agent posture, `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Define mandatory hygiene controls per asset class; (2) normalise hostnames between CMDB and Tenable; (3) weekly remediation campaigns for failing assets; (4) escalate chronic failures to service owners; (5) include scorecard in board security briefing.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv
| join asset_fqdn [
    search index=tenable sourcetype="tenable:sc:assets" earliest=-1d
    | eval mfa_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)mfa|okta.verify"),1,0)
    | eval edr_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike"),1,0)
    | stats latest(mfa_ok) as mfa_ok latest(edr_ok) as edr_ok by dns_name
    | rename dns_name as asset_fqdn
]
| eval hygiene_failures=if(mfa_ok==0,1,0) + if(edr_ok==0,1,0)
| where hygiene_failures > 0
| table asset_fqdn, business_service, mfa_ok, edr_ok, hygiene_failures
```

Understanding this SPL

**NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Practices — Baseline Control Compliance (Art. 21(2)(g))** — Basic cyber hygiene underpins all Article 21 measures. This use case scores endpoints and servers for MFA enrollment, disk encryption, EDR presence, and secure configuration baselines — rolling up to a hygiene scorecard per business service in NIS2 scope.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security` (4624 with MFA claims where available), `OktaIM2:log`, `tenable:was` or agent posture, `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hygiene_failures** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where hygiene_failures > 0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Practices — Baseline Control Compliance (Art. 21(2)(g))** — Basic cyber hygiene underpins all Article 21 measures. This use case scores endpoints and servers for MFA enrollment, disk encryption, EDR presence, and secure configuration baselines — rolling up to a hygiene scorecard per business service in NIS2 scope.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security` (4624 with MFA claims where available), `OktaIM2:log`, `tenable:was` or agent posture, `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: asset_fqdn, business_service, edr_ok, hygiene_failures, mfa_ok.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (failures by control), Table (worst assets), Single value (% compliant fleet), Treemap (by department).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for basic cyber hygiene underpins all article 21 measures. this use case scores endpoints and servers for mfa enrollment, disk encryption, edr presence, and secure configuration baselines — rolling up. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
